annotations.label: Picard–Lindelöf theorem (local existence and uniqueness)
annotations.note: Mathlib bundles the rectangle/Lipschitz/continuity hypotheses into `IsPicardLindelof` and concludes existence of a solution α with HasDerivWithinAt on `Icc tmin tmax`. Uniqueness is the companion theorem `ODE_solution_unique` in `Mathlib.Analysis.ODE.Gronwall`.

annotations.label: Initial value problem (IVP)
annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `IVP` structure; an IVP is expressed via the pattern `α t₀ = y₀ ∧ ∀ t, HasDerivAt α (f t (α t)) t` (as in the conclusion of `exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt`).

annotations.label: Solutions of the ODE satisfy the integral equation

anchors.snippet: any solution to the differential equation must also satisfy

annotations.note: Mathlib's `picard_eq_of_hasDerivAt` proves exactly this implication: if α has the right derivative on [t₀, t], then `picard f t₀ (α t₀) α t = α t` (i.e. α equals its own Picard image). The converse is `hasDerivWithinAt_picard_Icc`, and `IsPicardLindelof.FunSpace.isFixedPt_next_iff` states the iff directly via fixed-point form.

annotations.label: Picard operator Γ
annotations.note: `ODE.picard f t₀ x₀ α t = x₀ + ∫ τ in t₀..t, f τ (α τ)` is exactly this operator — Mathlib's `picard` evaluates Γα at time t given α : ℝ → E. (The Proof-sketch formula is the recurrence φ_{k+1} = Γφ_k; the Detailed-proof formula is the operator definition Γφ.) The 'Picard iteration' wikilink in the prose above refers to the more general fixed-point-iteration method.

annotations.label: Picard iterates converge to a solution of the IVP

anchors.from: It follows from the Banach fixed-point theorem that the sequence

annotations.note: `ContractingWith.tendsto_iterate_fixedPoint` gives the abstract statement: iterates of a contraction map on a complete metric space converge to its unique fixed point. Combined with `IsPicardLindelof.FunSpace.isFixedPt_next_iff` (which equates fixed points of the Picard operator with solutions to the integral equation), this yields the article's claim: lim_{k→∞} Γᵏφ₀ = a solution of the IVP. Mathlib's `IsPicardLindelof.exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt` uses this internally to produce the solution.

annotations.label: Banach fixed-point theorem (contraction mapping theorem)
annotations.note: The named-decl `ContractingWith.fixedPoint` returns the unique fixed point; `ContractingWith.exists_fixedPoint` and `ContractingWith.fixedPoint_unique` provide existence/uniqueness. Mathlib's statement is on an `EMetricSpace`.

annotations.label: Picard operator is a contraction (after restricting the interval)
annotations.note: Mathlib proves that some iterate `(next hf hx)^[n]` is a `ContractingWith C`, which suffices to apply Banach fixed-point and is strictly more general than directly contracting Γ.
annotations.proof_note: Article first shrinks the interval so that aL<1 to make Γ itself a contraction; Mathlib instead shows that some iterate Γⁿ is a contraction (matching the article's `Optimization of the solution's interval` step) and applies the iterate-corollary.

annotations.label: Iterate bound for the Picard operator (Γᵐ)
annotations.note: Mathlib proves `dist ((next)^[n] α t) ((next)^[n] β t) ≤ (K·|t−t₀|)ⁿ / n! · dist α β`, which is the article's lemma `‖Γᵐφ₁(t)−Γᵐφ₂(t)‖ ≤ Lᵐ|t−t₀|ᵐ/m! · ‖φ₁−φ₂‖`.

annotations.label: Iterate corollary of Banach fixed-point (Tⁿ contraction ⇒ T has unique fixed point)
annotations.note: Used in Mathlib's Picard-Lindelöf proof at `FunSpace.exists_isFixedPt_next` to turn a contracting iterate into a fixed point of the original map.

annotations.label: Picard–Lindelöf for C¹ vector fields (existence of integral curve)
annotations.note: A `C¹` (hence locally Lipschitz) vector field on a Banach space admits a local solution to the IVP on an open interval `Ioo (t₀−ε) (t₀+ε)`.

annotations.label: Peano existence theorem
annotations.note: Existence (without uniqueness) for an IVP whose right-hand side is only continuous in y (not Lipschitz). No Mathlib decl for Peano existence was located.

annotations.label: Counter-example: dy/dt = y^{1/3} (continuous but not Lipschitz) has three solutions
annotations.note: The IVP dy/dt = y^{1/3}, y(0)=0 has continuous but not Lipschitz right-hand side. Three explicit solutions exist: y(t)=0 and y(t)=±(2t/3)^{3/2}, illustrating that the Lipschitz hypothesis of Picard–Lindelöf is needed for uniqueness. Mathlib has no formalization of this counter-example.

annotations.label: Carathéodory's existence theorem
annotations.note: Existence under weaker conditions on f (measurable in t, continuous in y). No Mathlib formalization was located.

annotations.label: Okamura's theorem (necessary & sufficient uniqueness criterion)
annotations.note: Gives necessary and sufficient conditions for uniqueness of IVP solutions. Not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Global existence for globally Lipschitz vector fields
annotations.note: Mathlib's ODE files give only local existence (`IsPicardLindelof.exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt`); no theorem stating that a global Lipschitz bound yields a solution on all of ℝ was found.

annotations.label: Counter-example: locally Lipschitz dy/dt = y² blows up in finite time

anchor.from: If f is only locally Lipschitz

annotations.note: Shows that local Lipschitz (or even smoothness) is not enough for global existence: the IVP dy/dt = y², y(0)=1 has solution y(t)=1/(1−t), undefined at t=1. Mathlib has the local existence theorems but no formalisation of this specific blow-up counter-example.

annotations.label: Integral curves on a compact manifold exist for all time
annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes LOCAL existence and uniqueness of integral curves of a `C^∞` vector field on a manifold (`exists_isMIntegralCurveAt_of_contMDiffAt`), but the global statement specific to compact manifolds — that every trajectory of a smooth vector field on a compact manifold exists for all time — was not located in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Counter-example: non-uniqueness when Lipschitz fails (dy/dt = a·y^{2/3})
annotations.note: The IVP dy/dt = a·y^{2/3}, y(0)=0 admits two distinct solutions (y(t)=0 and y(t)=(at/3)^3), showing that the Lipschitz hypothesis of Picard–Lindelöf is necessary for uniqueness. Mathlib formalizes the positive direction (Picard–Lindelöf, ContractingWith etc.) but has no counter-examples for the failure case — this specific non-uniqueness instance has not been formalized.
